A Memoir on Survival, Sacrifice, and Healing
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Act I — Born into the Storm
I was born in a stormy July, past 3 AM on the groovy 70s. As the eldest of six whole siblings and ten half-siblings which do not include miscarriage, abortion or those that were kept secret, my life was defined by the tempest before I even understood what peace meant.
When I was just seven years old, the heavy, crushing burden of a parent was forced onto my shoulders. I had to become the adult, the caregiver, and the protector of my siblings while my mother was away trying to track down my father.
I grew up in constant fear. I watched my parents try to kill each other in front of me during their violent, terrifying fights. My father was a severe drunk and a womaniser. Because there was no safe guidance or protection at home, I became an easy target for predators.
When I was young, I was molested by my own uncles and a cousin. Those horrific memories still flash back regularly, bringing back the nightmare. At fifteen, a man on a bicycle sexually harassed me on the street, grabbing my breast as he rode by. I was stalked by men. I lived in a perpetual state of hypervigilance, keeping my guard up at all times just to keep my siblings safe.
I chose to live with absolutely no vices. I never drank, and I never strayed into addiction. I fought to be a flawless role model. Yet, my reward for being the responsible one was total exploitation. When I was a child, my parents literally cracked open my piggy banks to take my small coin savings. When I was a teenager, my father took the precious, irreplaceable jewelry given to me by my great-grandmother and pawned it away without my consent.
To keep a roof over our heads, I fought a brutal, multi-year legal battle to transfer a property to my name so no one could take it from us. I had to sell my own personal belongings and jewelry, emptying my bank accounts until I had nothing left, just to give to a family that only knew how to take.
The Lesson I Learned — When a family relies entirely on one person to fix everything, they get used to just receiving. They forget that the provider is a human being with limits who gets exhausted. Kindness is a gift to be cherished, not a mine to be stripped until it completely collapses.

Act II — The OFW Grind - “Out of Sight, Out of Mind”
After turning just eighteen, I became an Overseas Foreign Worker (OFW). For decades, I dragged myself across the globe, managing the brutal logistics of packing up my life to live and work entirely on my own in several parts of Asia and North America, before moving to India for marriage, and eventually — to the United Kingdom
During all those years working abroad, there was no comfort waiting for me. No one was there at home to cook a hot meal for me, or to simply hand me a cup of tea when I returned from an exhausting, brutal shift. I handled every chore, every move, and every heavy lift completely on my own — even immediately after major hospital surgeries.
Despite not finishing an elite degree in college and working in fields completely unaligned with my background, I fought like a warrior in the corporate world. I rose from a plant operator to an Organisational Development Partner for global giants.
I worked daily to pure exhaustion to feed up to twenty people and build homes for them back home. But because I was far away, I became “out of sight, out of mind” to them.
The Lesson I Learned — In our culture, migrant workers are often treated like human ATMs. Because families back home cannot physically see the skipped meals, the deep loneliness, the overtime, or the physical toll required to earn foreign currency, they casualise the sacrifice. They assume you are living a life of luxury just because you are abroad. Material things mean absolutely nothing if the provider’s own life and strength are being systemically drained from afar.

Act III — The Myth of Reciprocity and Family Loyalty
One of the hardest and most painful lessons I had to learn is this: never expect that when you give your life for people, they will return it with respect, care, or love. The sad truth of human nature is that people have incredibly short memories for the good you did. They will quickly forget years of your blood, sweat, and financial sacrifice. The moment you are empty and can no longer provide, they will focus only on your flaws or any minor inconvenience you cause them.
Worse, some will choose to retaliate against you, completely erasing everything you built for them. When a conflict arises, no matter how much you starved yourself so they could eat, they will never stand up for you. They will always choose to protect, listen to, and side with their own immediate spouses and children, pushing you to the side like a stranger.
The Lesson I Learned — Blood does not guarantee loyalty, and sacrifice does not buy respect. When people build their own lives using your sacrifices as their foundation, they will protect their immediate circles first — even if it means trampling on you. You cannot expect transactional love from a dysfunctional system. You must learn to save yourself, because no one is coming to save the savior.

Act IV — The Body Truly Keeps the Score
When you spend your entire life in a state of chronic stress, your internal compass for safety gets damaged. Because of my childhood traumas, I chose life partners who were wrong for me. My former husband was a cocaine addict, a drunk, and a womaniser who literally beat me to the point of death. Other partners cheated on me and betrayed my trust.
My physical body has been thoroughly battered. I have been bitten by dogs and insects, surviving three minor incidents that required stitches alongside major accidents that required full hospitalisation. Yet, even immediately after surgeries, I was left to lift heavy objects, cook for crowds, and wash laundry for houseguests.
My current marriage has also depleted my energy. My husband deals with his own anxiety and depression but delays to get the proper professional help, resulting in erratic behavior. Finding out that he invested his emotional connection and attention into other women instead of focusing on our marriage re-traumatised my already broken heart. For three long years, I have lived with no intimacy and even simple caresses — starved of the basic human affection needed to release the happy hormones that naturally buffer physical pain.
Then came the final power surge. In a span of just two to three years, I suffered “bereavement overload.” I lost seven people close to me, including my brother, my mother, my father-in-law I was very close with and treated me like his own, my uncles, and my close friends. This grief sits right on top of the old, unhealed memories of losing my aunt, grandmother and great-grandmother.
The machine finally blew its fuses. In the face of this heavy accumulation of trauma and sadness, I reached a point where I had daily thoughts of wanting to end my life — not because I am bitter, but because my mind and body are utterly crushed under too much weight.
Today, I am physically failing due to a severe illness with advanced eye, nerve, and major organ complications. The constant physical nerve pain prevents me from sleeping, meaning my machine can never shut down to repair itself.
The Lesson I Learned — You cannot abuse and overwork a machine for decades without it breaking down. Severe chronic illness is the physical toll of long-term emotional neglect and a lack of self-care. Caring for yourself is not a luxury or a selfish act; it is a medical necessity to stay alive. If you do not set boundaries and stop giving, your body will eventually force a shutdown for you.

Act V — The Final Chapter: Lowering the Shield
I am writing my story today because I am finally dropping my shield. I am done pretending to be strong. I have done everything humanly and scientifically possible — reaching out to therapies, doctors, charities, healthier diet and lifestyle choices— and because I have reached the absolute limit of my human strength, I am now surrendering the outcomes to God.
I do not want anyone’s judgment, quick solutions, or defensiveness. Some of the accountability is mine because I chose to over-give until I was empty. I also know that my siblings are victims of the exact same parents and lack of foundation. My husband is also carrying his own deep grief after losing my father-in-law, a man I loved dearly. Many people in this world have suffered just as much, or more.
But I am sharing this to raise awareness that these traumas happen in real life, not just in the movies. We need to talk about what happens when unplanned families have no moral grounds, when vices take over, and when children are exploited.
If you are currently the “strong one” in your family, remember that you are allowed to stop. Lower your guard, protect your boundaries, and demand small, gentle steps of care from those around you before your machine completely shuts down.
Written by Z.W. Meags based on a true story of their life
